ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Prenatal Assessment & Routine Care
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Prenatal Assessment and Routine Care | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Prenatal Assessment and Routine Care — Maternity. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Prenatal Assessment & Routine Care** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Prenatal Assessment & Routine Care** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items cluster **dating**, **risk screening**, **nutrition and teratogen teaching**, **IPV** and mental health themes when shown, and **referral** when red flags appear. Expect to prioritize **objective data collection** before reassurance alone.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **History**: obstetric history, medications, allergies, immunizations, social determinants when the stem lists them.
- **Physical**: fundal height themes, fetal heart rate auscultation, blood pressure trends, edema, and warning-sign education.
- **Scope**: support ordered labs and referrals; nursing focuses on **assessment, teaching, and escalation** rather than independent diagnosis.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **first prenatal visit with uncertain last menstrual period** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**

- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Gestational Diabetes
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Gestational Diabetes | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Gestational Diabetes — Maternity. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Gestational Diabetes** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Gestational Diabetes** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items test **diet and activity teaching**, **glucose monitoring**, **insulin safety** if ordered, **fetal surveillance** themes, and **hypoglycemia** after medication or missed meals.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Nutrition**: carbohydrate consistency; dietitian referral when ordered.
- **Birth planning**: macrosomia and **shoulder dystocia** risk when stem cues large baby or polyhydramnios.
- **Postpartum**: type 2 diabetes screening teaching for long-term health.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **elevated one-hour glucose screen with follow-up oral glucose tolerance testing planned** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Amniotic Fluid Complications** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Amniotic Fluid Complications** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Boards tie **polyhydramnios** to **diabetes**, **fetal anomalies**, and **preterm ROM** risk; **oligohydramnios** to **growth restriction**, **ruptured membranes**, and **postdates surveillance**—pick teaching and monitoring aligned with the stem.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **NST/BPP** themes when surveillance is ordered for fluid abnormalities.
- **Position changes** and oxygen for **non-reassuring status** when the stem pairs fluid issues with tracing changes.
- **Patient education**: report decreased movement, leaking fluid, or tightening patterns promptly.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **fundal height larger than dates with polyhydramnios on ultrasound report** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:
Examiners use **Uterine Tachysystole** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Expect **stop or reduce oxytocin per order**, **left lateral positioning**, **IV fluid bolus per protocol**, **terbutaline** or other tocolytic themes when ordered, and **continuous FHR monitoring** until contractions normalize.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **FHR** must be interpreted with contraction frequency—tachysystole plus **late decels** is urgent.
- **Document** contraction count and intervention response.
- **Teaching**: oxytocin is not self-adjusted; nurse follows titration protocols strictly.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **more than five contractions in ten minutes after oxytocin increase** brings you to the bedside. **Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. **Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Oxytocin Nursing Care — Maternity. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Oxytocin Nursing Care** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Oxytocin Nursing Care** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

NCLEX stresses **line piggyback safety**, **titration per protocol**, **tachysystole response**, **FHR interpretation**, and **never bolus oxytocin** outside explicit order scenarios designed to be wrong answers.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Primary nurse** stays with high-risk titration per facility policy when the stem emphasizes safety.
- **Fluid balance**: watch for water intoxication themes when prolonged high-dose infusion is described.
- **Postpartum**: second-line uterotonic use only per order after hemorrhage—not independent dosing.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **oxytocin infusion titrated upward with escalating contraction frequency** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Uterine Rupture** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Uterine Rupture** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Choose **emergency delivery preparation**, **large-bore IV access**, **notify team**, **continuous monitoring**, and **avoid further uterotonic stimulation** when rupture is suspected—never delay for routine tasks.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **VBAC or scarred uterus** risk when the stem highlights prior cesarean incision type.
- **Differentiate** overlapping emergencies using stem cues; both abruption and rupture demand rapid response.
- **Postpartum** hemorrhage readiness and massive transfusion protocol themes when shown.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **sudden severe abdominal pain with loss of fetal station and bradycardia during labour** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Postpartum Depression and Psychosis** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Postpartum Depression and Psychosis** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Safety first** for **postpartum psychosis** or **homicidal or suicidal ideation**: **do not leave infant alone with parent**, **activate crisis and provider pathways**, **therapeutic communication without judgment**—outpatient counselling alone is wrong when acute danger appears.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Screening** tools at routine postpartum visits when the stem references them.
- **Support person** involvement for sleep and safety planning when appropriate.
- **Medications**: antidepressant onset delay and adherence teaching when stable.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **new parent reports inability to bond and intrusive thoughts of harming the baby** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Newborn Jaundice and Phototherapy** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Newborn Jaundice and Phototherapy** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items test **eye protection**, **temperature stability**, **hydration and stool frequency**, **skin integrity under lights**, and **kernicterus red flags** (poor feeding, arching, high-pitched cry) requiring urgent escalation.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Breastfeeding jaundice** versus **pathologic** timing cues in vignettes.
- **Exchange transfusion** thresholds when stem lists extreme values—notify team urgently.
- **Home phototherapy** safety teaching only when stable outpatient context is described.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **total bilirubin crossing phototherapy threshold on day three with good feeding** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Maternal Priority Questions: What Do You Do First?** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Maternal Priority Questions: What Do You Do First?** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Pick **seizure safety**, **airway**, **magnesium and antihypertensive pathways**, **hemorrhage**, **cord prolapse**, or **uterine rupture** over stable teaching—NCLEX uses **unstable versus stable** triage repeatedly.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Call for help** and **time-critical meds** only per orders and scope.
- **Eliminate** answers that chart first or finish paperwork when a life threat is present.
- **Pair vitals** with fetal status whenever labour complications appear.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **four competing tasks including one client with eclamptic seizure and another with stable routine discharge teaching** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**

- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Gravidity & Parity** (Maternity) links antepartum risk screening, intrapartum maternal-fetal surveillance, immediate postpartum stabilization, and newborn transition to safe nursing judgment: recognize hemorrhage, severe hypertension and seizure risk, non-reassuring fetal status patterns, cord emergencies, infection, and neonatal respiratory or metabolic compromise, then escalate per policy and orders.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **unstable vs stable**, **delegation**, and **first action** sequencing.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Gravidity & Parity** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

NCLEX tests **GTPAL/TPAL** interpretation, implications of **multiparity** or **grand multiparity**, and **prior cesarean** or **postpartum hemorrhage** history that changes surveillance when shown.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Gravidity** counts pregnancies including the current pregnancy unless the stem defines otherwise.
- **Parity** follows the notation system printed in the chart; do not invent numbers not documented.
- **Risk teaching**: link parity history to **preterm labour**, **placenta issues**, or **VBAC counselling** only when the vignette supports it.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **admission record reads G3P2 at the start of labour** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- Integrate **maternal vitals, uterine tone, lochia, pain, fetal tracing, and newborn work of breathing** to identify priority threats across the perinatal continuum.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, and unit protocol for labour, OR recovery, and postpartum or newborn liaison themes when shown.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **severe hypertension**, **eclampsia**, **magnesium toxicity**, **obstetric hemorrhage**, **uterine rupture**, **shoulder dystocia**, **cord prolapse**, **sepsis**, **respiratory failure**, or **critical newborn hypoglycemia**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Placenta Previa** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**No routine vaginal examination** unless explicit delivery-readiness context; prepare for **hemorrhage**, **type and screen**, **continuous fetal monitoring**, and **positioning** per policy.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Expectant management**: activity and bleeding precautions when stable per stem.
- **Shock**: large-bore IV access, oxygen, rapid team activation when vitals deteriorate.
- **Teaching**: call emergency services for heavy bleeding, syncope, or decreased fetal movement.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **painless bright red vaginal bleeding at thirty-two weeks gestation** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.

Examiners use **Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring Basics** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items test **baseline**, **variability**, **accelerations**, **uterine activity**, and **reassuring versus non-reassuring** framing—choose **position change**, **IV fluid bolus per order**, **oxygen per protocol**, and **notify** when escalation criteria appear.

Eliminate answers that **skip assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show risk, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Reposition** the labouring patient and correct hypotension when epidural-related.
- **Avoid** routine high-flow oxygen without indication; follow the stem and policy cues.
- **Document** tracing changes objectively after interventions.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **category II tracing with minimal variability after epidural placement** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the maternity topic hub (**`maternity`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Postpartum hemorrhage](LESSON:postpartum-hemorrhage) · [Late decelerations & FHR](LESSON:late-decelerations-fhr) · [Preeclampsia vs eclampsia](LESSON:preeclampsia-vs-eclampsia) · [Newborn thermoregulation & feeding](LESSON:newborn-thermoregulation-feeding) · [Rh incompatibility basics](LESSON:rh-incompatibility-basics) · [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating mother or newborn.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Safety**: fall prevention when legs are heavy; bed low and call bell within reach.
- **Bladder**: urinary retention assessment per protocol.
- **Spinal headache** teaching for post-dural puncture symptoms when follow-up care appears.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A perinatal client scenario related to **hypotension and fetal bradycardia shortly after epidural bolus** brings you to the bedside. You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **late decelerations with loss of variability**, **acute abdomen with fetal bradycardia**, **prolapsed cord with bradycardia**, **shoulder dystocia after head delivery**, or **postpartum fundus that fails to contract with heavy bleeding**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match your unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **maternal vitals + uterine tone + lochia + pain + fetal status** (and **newborn tone, color, RR, glucose** when relevant) before routine tasks when risk is rising.

- **Recovery**: fundus, lochia, pain, incision, and early mobilization per pathway.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Maternal bleeding** with hypotension, tachycardia, or altered mental status → activate hemorrhage pathway per protocol; large-bore access and type and screen themes when shown.
- **Severe-range blood pressure**, **headache with visual changes**, **RUQ pain**, **hyperreflexia**, or **seizure** → preeclampsia/eclampsia cluster; magnesium and antihypertensive safety when ordered.
- **Magnesium toxicity** clues: absent DTRs, respiratory depression, extreme somnolence → stop infusion per order/policy and prepare emergency support.
- **Non-reassuring FHR**, **uterine tachysystole**, **loss of station**, **sudden severe pain** → intrapartum emergency mindset until ruled out.
- **Newborn grunting, nasal flaring, retractions**, **cyanosis**, **apnea**, **temperature instability**, or **feeding refusal with jitteriness** → escalate newborn assessment per protocol.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.
- Reinforce **medication safety**, **breastfeeding or formula preparation basics**, **safe sleep on a firm flat surface**, and **follow-up timing** only as orders and readiness allow.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:

You pair **focused maternal and fetal assessment** (or **newborn assessment** when the stem is post-birth) with **vitals, labs, and tracing review** when provided, maintain **airway and perfusion**, protect **bleeding and infection control**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually reassessment plus escalation for **hemorrhage**, **severe hypertension/seizure**, **cord prolapse**, **shoulder dystocia**, **uterine rupture**, **sepsis**, or **newborn respiratory failure**—rather than routine documentation or delayed reporting alone.

- Teach **warning signs** (bleeding, fever, severe headache, decreased fetal movement, fluid gush, regular contractions before term) and **when to call emergency services** versus same-day clinic.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid comfort-only or teaching-first answers when instability is present.
- When multiple patients compete, pick the mother-baby pair who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:

**Red flags / safety alerts**
